candidate.definition: Context rot is when users spend more time crafting defensive prompts to route around stale context than the AI saves on the task—turning every interaction into "context archaeology" where you explain what to ignore before you can explain what to do.
candidate.manifestation: A marketing manager at a 35-person B2B SaaS company uses Claude to generate email campaigns. Each request begins with a defensive preamble: "Ignore last month's persona definitions—we pivoted to enterprise. Don't use the old pricing tiers. Use the new messaging framework from my notes, not the one you remember from three threads ago." The clarification often takes longer than drafting the email manually.
candidate.cost_model: For a 25-person team where each member spends 3 minutes per AI request writing defensive prompts to route around stale context, averaging 8 requests per day: 25 × 3 min × 8 × 250 days = 2,500 hours annually at $50/hour fully-loaded = $125,000 in prompt overhead.
candidate.inevitability: As AI adoption grows, conversational history accumulates across more tools and sessions, requiring increasingly elaborate prompting rituals just to route around outdated context. Prompt engineering scales linearly with context volume—the more you use AI, the more time you spend explaining what *not* to do.
candidate.counter_narrative: Prompt engineering and careful few-shot examples can steer the AI around stale context in a single session, but they can't fix structural memory fragmentation. Uncle Bob observed: "One of the problems with a big context window is that it remembers too much." No prompt can tell the AI what to forget when stale information accumulates faster than users can manually invalidate it.

verdict.legibility_reason: "Context archaeology" vividly names the pain; a VP of Ops would instantly recognize explaining-what-to-ignore as dead time.
verdict.economic_reason: Full cost chain with verifiable math: 3 min/request × 8 requests/day × 25 people × 250 days = $125k annually.
verdict.inevitability_reason: Structural claim that prompt overhead scales linearly with context volume; Uncle Bob citation validates; lacks quantitative studies.
verdict.counter_narrative_reason: Names prompt engineering fix, cites Uncle Bob on "remembers too much," explains why manual invalidation can't keep pace.
